**Session 2022-23**

chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written evidence submitted by James Wilson (OSB78)**

**Submission to the Public Bill Committee on the Online safety Bill.**

Dear Committee Members,

I am writing to register my strong opposition to the Online Safety Bill, which – in my view – is ill-conceived, and will, if enacted, have damaging consequences for freedom of speech and a flourishing civil society.

It seems strange to be saying this to a Parliamentary Committee, but here goes: the liberties we enjoy in the U.K. – evolved and refined over centuries, and for which hundreds of thousands of people have died – are founded on respect for the law, and the belief that we are all equal before it. That requires absolute clarity about what constitutes an offence, so that, if they are presented with enough evidence, the members of a jury have no choice but to find the accused guilty – while, if the evidence is insufficient, they are equally obliged to find him/her *not* guilty.
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written evidence submitted by James Wilson (OSB78)**

**Submission to the Public Bill Committee on the Online safety Bill.**

Dear Committee Members,

I am writing to register my strong opposition to the Online Safety Bill, which – in my view – is ill-conceived, and will, if enacted, have damaging consequences for freedom of speech and a flourishing civil society.

It seems strange to be saying this to a Parliamentary Committee, but here goes: the liberties we enjoy in the U.K. – evolved and refined over centuries, and for which hundreds of thousands of people have died – are founded on respect for the law, and the belief that we are all equal before it. That requires absolute clarity about what constitutes an offence, so that, if they are presented with enough evidence, the members of a jury have no choice but to find the accused guilty – while, if the evidence is insufficient, they are equally obliged to find him/her *not* guilty.

chunks.text: before it. That requires absolute clarity about what constitutes an offence, so that, if they are presented with enough evidence, the members of a jury have no choice but to find the accused guilty – while, if the evidence is insufficient, they are equally obliged to find him/her *not* guilty. The Online Safety Bill undermines this essential precondition for the rule of law, by using terms – "Hate"; "Distress"; "Harm", etc. – that are intrinsically subjective, and so insusceptible to an agreed objective definition. One person’s "hate" is another person’s strongly-held opinion, and a third person’s joke. We have already seen, through the activities of organizations such as Stop Funding Hate, how these vague abstract terms can be used to silence or "punish" the expression of perfectly legal views. (SFH, for instance, publicly accused Richard Littlejohn of "hate" when he suggested that children raised by a gay couple might suffer in comparison with those brought up by heterosexual parents. Of course, it would have been entirely legitimate to challenge his views, and adduce evidence – if it exists – to show that they are ill-founded. But to dismiss them under the emotive blanket term "hate" is effectively to remove them from the open debate that is at the heart of a free democratic society.)
chunks.display_text: The Online Safety Bill undermines this essential precondition for the rule of law, by using terms – "Hate"; "Distress"; "Harm", etc. – that are intrinsically subjective, and so insusceptible to an agreed objective definition. One person’s "hate" is another person’s strongly-held opinion, and a third person’s joke. We have already seen, through the activities of organizations such as Stop Funding Hate, how these vague abstract terms can be used to silence or "punish" the expression of perfectly legal views. (SFH, for instance, publicly accused Richard Littlejohn of "hate" when he suggested that children raised by a gay couple might suffer in comparison with those brought up by heterosexual parents. Of course, it would have been entirely legitimate to challenge his views, and adduce evidence – if it exists – to show that they are ill-founded. But to dismiss them under the emotive blanket term "hate" is effectively to remove them from the open debate that is at the heart of a free democratic society.)

chunks.text: been entirely legitimate to challenge his views, and adduce evidence – if it exists – to show that they are ill-founded. But to dismiss them under the emotive blanket term "hate" is effectively to remove them from the open debate that is at the heart of a free democratic society.) The bill’s supporters, I know, assure us that it will contain effective safeguards to prevent this kind of abuse. But the safeguards are themselves couched in such vague terms – "due regard to"; "content of democratic importance", etc. – as to be almost worthless. Perhaps the current government is high-minded enough to refrain from exploiting these provisions to protect its own interests; but it isn’t hard to imagine a future administration using its considerable power and resources to, e.g., contest whether a critical piece of journalism is "of democratic importance". And then there are the "legal but harmful" clauses. Surely it is contradictory in a democracy to threaten to fine someone for doing something that is not against the law? This lack of precision will, inevitably, only intensify the atmosphere of fear, suspicion and resentment that organizations like Stop Funding Hate are already creating. People will increasingly self-censor, terrified of attracting the attention of outraged activists or of a government bent on silencing dissent. I don’t doubt that this is not the bill’s intention, but you have to be remarkably lacking in human insight not to see that it will be the effect. And the only way to avoid it is to be absolutely clear: *To say the following is against the law. To say anything else is perfectly legal (though, of course, it may attract criticism) – and anyone who attempts to intimidate you into* not *saying it is trying to limit your freedom of speech.*
chunks.display_text: The bill’s supporters, I know, assure us that it will contain effective safeguards to prevent this kind of abuse. But the safeguards are themselves couched in such vague terms – "due regard to"; "content of democratic importance", etc. – as to be almost worthless. Perhaps the current government is high-minded enough to refrain from exploiting these provisions to protect its own interests; but it isn’t hard to imagine a future administration using its considerable power and resources to, e.g., contest whether a critical piece of journalism is "of democratic importance". And then there are the "legal but harmful" clauses. Surely it is contradictory in a democracy to threaten to fine someone for doing something that is not against the law? This lack of precision will, inevitably, only intensify the atmosphere of fear, suspicion and resentment that organizations like Stop Funding Hate are already creating. People will increasingly self-censor, terrified of attracting the attention of outraged activists or of a government bent on silencing dissent. I don’t doubt that this is not the bill’s intention, but you have to be remarkably lacking in human insight not to see that it will be the effect. And the only way to avoid it is to be absolutely clear: *To say the following is against the law. To say anything else is perfectly legal (though, of course, it may attract criticism) – and anyone who attempts to intimidate you into* not *saying it is trying to limit your freedom of speech.*

chunks.text: to avoid it is to be absolutely clear: *To say the following is against the law. To say anything else is perfectly legal (though, of course, it may attract criticism) – and anyone who attempts to intimidate you into* not *saying it is trying to limit your freedom of speech.* I accept that the advent of the Internet has created a situation which our pre-1990 legal and regulatory system could not have anticipated and was not designed to deal with. But finding a satisfactory way to respond to it demands a clarity and depth of thought, and an attention to protecting our freedoms, that this confused, dangerously naïve Bill does not display. Please think again. With thanks for your time, and best wishes, James Wilson ***10th June 2022***

Please think again. With thanks for your time, and best wishes, James Wilson ***10th June 2022***
